# Transcrição: 09- Stargazing Betrayal

- **Fonte:** 09- Stargazing Betrayal
- **Duração:** 05:49
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Let's bring to the stage Mr. Matthew Dix. I've been teaching elementary school for 16 years and I have learned many things over those years. I have learned that when a kid accidentally calls me mom, it is both a compliment and an insult. I have learned that I would much rather have a kid who throws the occasional chair at me than the sneaky one who bullies kids behind my back. And I have learned that no matter how many female teachers are in the general vicinity, there will be at least one female student every year who will come to me and tell me she has had her first period and I am the one who has to help her. But when I work with student teachers, college kids who are going to have their classrooms of their own someday, the thing I tell them first is they need to earn the trust of kids and they can never betray that trust. And I learned that lesson well the second year of my teaching career. I was teaching third grade at the time, I had 22 third graders, I was getting ready to start a unit on the sun, moon and earth, and I decided I was going to motivate these kids. So I told them I was going to have a stargazing night in my backyard. And if they worked hard, I was going to invite them to my stargazing night. If they did everything that I expected from them, they would be able to come. And that was enough to get them excited because to be able to come to a teacher's house after dark, that doesn't sound right, but to be able to do that for a kid is exciting. But it was my second year and I didn't know that was enough. So then I added that in addition to all the other things, I'd have a fire in the fire pit and we would roast marshmallows and have s'mores. And I have learned in teaching that you can get a kid to do anything for food. I once got a boy who hated theater to play Romeo and Romeo and Julia a one hour production for a can of Diet Coke. That should have been enough, but it was my second year. So I added, for anyone who is in attendance, I was going to take out my time machine and they were going to be able to use it. And eight years old is a beautiful age because they still believe in Santa. And if you are convincing enough, they will believe almost anything you say. And it was 1999, so there was no Google to prove me otherwise. And so those kids worked in ways you could never imagine. They made moon journals that rival Darwin's journals from the Beagle. They would be writing about the moon and if there was no moon, they would write poetry to the moon. And they would write the definition of the moon over and over and over again just to fill their journal with words to impress me. I remember Claire made a mobile of the solar system and she made the planets to scale, which if you know about the planets is really hard. So like Jupiter was a beach ball, but Earth was a cherry with a little toothpick stuck on the side with a pea for the moon. And she had left the sun off, I thought. I said, Claire, you don't have the sun and she said, no I do.

## [03:01 - 05:49]

And there was a little note card on the front, which I thought was her title card, but it said the sun and in parentheses underneath scale, Mr. Dix's ego. Right? So everyone was invited to Stargazing Night and they all came. And we looked at the moon through a telescope and we looked at the stars with star charts and we found Orion and the Big Dipper. And then I told the parents who had come to get the fire started and to start the s'mores and the kids were going to come to me to the field and I was going to take out the time machine. And these kids were like, they were like, if you string a guitar too tight and it's in a room and you clap, the guitar will ring, that's what these kids were like. They were shaking, they were quivering. And so I had them lay down in the field on the grass on their backs. And I told them to close their eyes and don't open them until I tell you to. And then I just sat and I looked at this field of kids just waiting in anticipation. And I said, okay, open your eyes and don't move. And they did. And I said, guys, the stars overhead, that light is thousands or millions of years old and you're looking into the past when you look at the sky. That's my time machine. Isn't it amazing? And I waited. And the first kid to speak was Daniel. He sat up and he said, I'm not going to see dinosaurs. And then I remember two girls stood up and they looked at me the way high school girls used to look at me. When they didn't like me back in high school, that look of like loud disdain without words. And they just walked towards the fire pit without saying a word. And then one by one, each one of these sweet eight year old children rose from the grass, turned their backs to me and just walked away without a word. And I was left standing in this field with my time machine all alone. And I knew what I had done. I had betrayed their trust and I felt awful. And so I waited a little bit. I was wondering what they were telling their parents and I was a little worried about that. And so finally I went over to the fire pit because I wanted some s'mores and they were all gathered around and it looked happy and whatever had happened I thought had been behind us. And so I went over to the parent and I said, you know, I'd love a s'more now. And one of the boys looked at me and he said, you have a time machine. You don't need a s'more. And so there was no reward for me that night. But I learned a very valuable lesson. Thank you.
